plainMeaning: In this closing crowning verse the poet praises the ninety-six Jinas of all three worlds and every eternal and built-in-time Jina-image, names his order and its teacher, and prays that whoever recites this finds growing prosperity and joy.
recitation: kalaśa | chhannue jinavara chhannue jinavara, adho ūrdhva ne loka tīrcche jāṇue. sāsaya asāsaya jina-paḍimā, te savve vakhāṇuṁ e. gaccha vidhipakṣa pūjya pragaṭa, śrī-dharma-mūrti sūrīndue. vācaka mūlā kahe bhaṇatāṁ, ṛddhi-vṛddhi āṇandue.

wordByWord.gloss: the crowning concluding stanza (heading marking this final 'pouring' verse)
wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit kalaśa, 'pitcher, finial'; here the section-label for the sealing benediction
wordByWord.notes: A rubric/heading printed before the stanza, not a syntactic part of it; retained verbatim.

wordByWord.etymology: from Prakrit channaviṁ / chha-ṇaüi < Sanskrit ṣaṇṇavati 'ninety-six'; -e is an oblique/locative-flavoured Old-Gujarati ending
wordByWord.notes: Refers to the past, present, future and Mahāvideha sets of twenty-four (4×24).
The doubling 'chhannue jinavar chhannue jinavar' is a chant-refrain device of devotional intensification.
Here gathers the three lokas into one sweep.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit tiriccha / tiyacchа < Sanskrit tiryak 'transverse, horizontal'; locative -e
Prakrit jāṇaï < Sanskrit jñā 'to know'; Old-Gujarati optative/euphonic -ue
wordByWord.notes: Invites the reciter to mentally apprehend the Jinas filling all three worlds.

wordByWord.etymology: jina + Prakrit paḍimā < Sanskrit pratimā 'image, icon'
wordByWord.notes: Both eternal and built-in-time icons are folded into a single act of praise.

wordByWord.etymology: Old-Gujarati vakhāṇavuṁ < Prakrit vakkhāṇa- < Sanskrit vyākhyāna 'exposition, praise'; first-person
wordByWord.notes: The poet himself becomes the devotee, summing the whole hymn in one verb of praise.
Recurring end-of-line filler that carries the melody; not separately translatable.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit gaccha < Sanskrit gaccha 'a monastic group'

wordByWord.notes: The gaccha's own designation, as set out in the preface.

wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit pūjya, gerundive of pūj 'to worship'

wordByWord.notes: Qualifies the gaccha as conspicuously honoured.

wordByWord.etymology: honorific śrī + the personal name Dharmamūrti ('embodiment of dharma')
wordByWord.notes: The gacchādhipati under whom the author wrote; named here to seal the work under his authority.

wordByWord.etymology: sūri 'ācārya, preceptor' + indu/indra 'lord, chief'; Old-Gujarati -ue ending
wordByWord.notes: Title of pre-eminence; the -indu form also evokes 'moon', a stock simile of radiant glory.

wordByWord.gloss: Vācaka (the Upādhyāya rank; the author's title)

wordByWord.etymology: proper name; the closing poetic signature (bhaṇitā)
wordByWord.notes: 'Vācaka Mūlā kahe' is the author's recurring signature refrain.

wordByWord.etymology: Old-Gujarati kahevuṁ < Prakrit kahaï < Sanskrit kath 'to tell'

wordByWord.gloss: while reciting / for those who recite
wordByWord.etymology: present participle of Prakrit bhaṇaï 'recites' < Sanskrit bhaṇ 'to speak, recite'
wordByWord.notes: Conditions the benediction on the act of recitation.

wordByWord.gloss: prosperity and increase (material and spiritual abundance)

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit āṇanda < Sanskrit ānanda 'bliss'; Old-Gujarati -ue ending
wordByWord.notes: The hymn ends on ānanda — the fruit promised to the reciter.

literalTranslation: Ninety-six excellent Jinas, ninety-six excellent Jinas — know them in the lower, the upper, and the middle world. Eternal and non-eternal Jina-images — all of those I extol. The Vidhipakṣa gaccha, venerable and renowned, the chief-of-preceptors Śrī Dharmamūrti(sūri); Vācaka Mūlā says: to those who recite, prosperity, increase and joy.
idiomaticTranslation: Ninety-six radiant Jinas — ninety-six radiant Jinas! — behold them filling the lower, the upper and the middle world. Every Jina-image, eternal or fashioned in time, I now extol with all my heart. Glory to the renowned and venerable Vidhipakṣa order and to its crown of preceptors, Śrī Dharmamūrtisūri. Vācaka Mūlā declares: whoever recites this hymn shall gather ever-growing prosperity, abundance and joy.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: The kalaśa does for the whole hymn what its name implies — it is the finial poured over the temple of all eight dhālas, gathering their immensity into a single breath of praise. The opening line collapses the entire enumeration that has gone before: the four caubīsīs become 'chhannue jinavar', and the doubling — 'chhannue jinavar, chhannue jinavar' — is not redundancy but chant, the refrain swelling so the reciter feels the number rather than counts it. The second half-line, 'adho ūrdhva ne loka tīrcche jāṇue', telescopes dhālas 5–7 (the lower, upper and middle worlds) into three words, asking the worshipper to 'know' — to hold in mind — Jinas distributed through the whole triloka. The next line resolves the hymn's running śāśvata/aśāśvata distinction into one inclusive act: 'sāsaya asāsaya jina-paḍimā, te savve vakhāṇuṁ' — eternal and made-in-time images alike, *all* of them, the poet extols. The verb shifts from the earlier 'jāṇue' (know) to 'vakhāṇuṁ' (I praise), moving the verse from cognition to devotion, and from the assembly to the single voice of the author. The last two lines turn from cosmos to lineage: the manifest, venerable Vidhipakṣa gaccha and its 'sūrīndu', Śrī Dharmamūrti — the work is signed and sealed under living authority. Finally the poetic colophon 'Vācaka Mūlā kahe' issues the phala-śruti: 'bhaṇatāṁ ṛddhi-vṛddhi āṇandue' — recitation yields prosperity, increase and joy. Thus the verse completes the hymn's characteristic motion from the boundless cosmos inward to the named teacher, and outward again to the reciter's own welfare.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: Two doctrinal threads are knotted here. First, the count 'ninety-six' encodes the Jain conviction that awakened guides are never absent: twenty-four for the past age, twenty-four for the present, twenty-four for the future, and twenty-four of the ever-present Mahāvideha realm — time and space are never void of a Jina. Second, the pairing 'sāsaya asāsaya jina-paḍimā' affirms a specifically Mūrtipūjaka teaching: the Jina is rightly venerated through his image (caitya-vandana), whether that image is one of the eternal, uncreated icons of the śāśvata-tīrthas or one consecrated by human hands in time — both are fit objects of reverence. Extolling 'te savve' (all of them) is therefore a quiet doctrinal assertion of image-worship as a means of strengthening samyaktva. The closing phala-śruti reflects the standard Jain logic of puṇya: reverent recitation accrues merit that ripens as ṛddhi, vṛddhi and ānanda — though the deeper aim, consonant with the hymn's awe-before-immensity, is the steadying of faith rather than worldly gain alone.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: Recited as the seal of the morning caityavandana, this verse lets the worshipper let go of the dizzying numbers just heard and rest in a single, whole-hearted bow to every Jina and every Jina-image, everywhere. A practitioner today can use the doubled 'chhannue jinavar' as a focusing refrain, then consciously include both the famous eternal tīrthas and the image in their own local temple under 'sāsaya asāsaya... te savve', and close by receiving the benediction of ṛddhi-vṛddhi-ānanda as an intention for the day rather than a transaction.

crossReferences.text: Logassa (Cauvīsattho) — praise of the twenty-four Tīrthaṅkaras, the nucleus the 'chhannu' expands fourfold
crossReferences.source: Panch Pratikraman Sūtra – Vidhi Sahit (Anchalgaccha/Vidhipaksha ed.)

crossReferences.text: Aṣṭottarī Tīrthamālā — the 108 tīrthas, parallel devotional census of śāśvata/aśāśvata shrines
Jaina triloka cosmography — adho-/ūrdhva-/tiryag-loka framework underlying 'adho ūrdhva... tīrcche'

sources.title: Shashvata, Śāśvata — definitions (incl. Jainism)
sources.consultedFor: Confirming śāśvata 'eternal/ever-existing' (Prakrit sāsaya) and its Jain usage for ever-existing entities.

sources.title: Kalasha, Kalaśa — definitions (incl. Jainism)
sources.consultedFor: Sense of kalaśa as a finial/crowning element, supporting the reading of the verse as the hymn's sealing benediction.

sources.title: Jinabimba, Jina-bimba — definitions
sources.consultedFor: Corroborating jina-image (paḍimā/bimba/pratimā) as 'icon/statue of a Jina'.

sources.title: Achal Gaccha (Anchalgachchha / Vidhipaksha)
sources.consultedFor: Identity of the Vidhipakṣa/Anchalgaccha named in the kalaśa as the author's order.

notes: The verse-initial 'કલશ' is a printed section-heading (the kalaśa rubric) rather than a syntactic word of the stanza; glossed but flagged as a label. Old-Gujarati line-final '-એ/-ઉએ' (jāṇue, sūrīndue, āṇandue) and the standalone 'એ' are euphonic/metrical endings whose exact grammatical force is soft; rendered as emphatic/optative per context. 'સૂરીંદુએ' is read as sūri-indra ('chief of preceptors'); the indu spelling additionally evokes 'moon' (a glory-simile), noted but not pressed. 'છન્નુએ' = 96, read per the hymn's own four-caubīsī scheme. Spellings (સાસય/અસાસય, જિનપડિમા, વખાણું) are kept verbatim per the source book's orthography. Dharmamūrtisūri's precise pontifical dates are not asserted, consistent with the preface's caution.
